ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for chicken katsu don; mapped to broader 'rice bowl dish' class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based dish with meat — composite cooked dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for chicken katsu don; A0CKM (composite rice dish with meat) is the closest defensible broader match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for chicken katsu don or equivalent Japanese rice bowl dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of chicken katsu don is approximately 350 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice topped with a deep-fried breaded chicken cutlet and egg simmered in a soy-dashi broth.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Katsu Don — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chicken Katsu Don (F05100019). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chicken katsu don / breaded fried chicken with rice (NDB No. 21322 analogue) as HPB does not report saturated fat separately; this is documented as an adapted value.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice-based mixed dish analogue
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken katsu don. GI of 72 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white jasmine/medium-grain cooked rice (GI ~72, high category), which constitutes the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. The breaded fried chicken and egg components contribute negligible additional carbohydrate relative to the rice base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 54.6) / 100 ≈ 39.3, using a 350 g serving with 15.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 358 mg per 100 g, primarily from soy sauce and dashi broth, with low potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200 g and request reduced soy sauce in the broth, as a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,253 mg sodium — more than half the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~72) with low dietary fibre (0.89 g per 100 g) produces a high glycaemic load of approximately 39 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit chicken katsu don due to its high glycaemic load; substituting brown rice, reducing the rice portion by half, and replacing the deep-fried katsu with grilled chicken can substantially lower postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation contributes saturated fat (~1.6 g per 100 g) and total fat 6.51 g per 100 g; high sodium (358 mg per 100 g) further elevates cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat chicken katsu don as an occasional food only, given its combination of deep-fried saturated fat, high sodium, and refined carbohydrate, all of which are independently associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken katsu don (チキンカツ丼) is a Japanese donburi (rice bowl) dish consisting of a deep-fried panko-breaded chicken cutlet (katsu) served over steamed white rice and topped with a soft-set egg simmered in a sweet-savoury broth of soy sauce, dashi, mirin, and onion. The dish is a chicken-based variant of the classic tonkatsu don (pork cutlet bowl) and is widely served in Japanese restaurants and food courts across Singapore and Southeast Asia. The chicken cutlet is typically made from breast or thigh meat, coated in panko breadcrumbs, and deep-fried until golden and crisp, then briefly simmered in the broth with beaten egg to create a cohesive topping. The result is a dish with contrasting textures — crispy breaded chicken against soft egg and fluffy rice — and a rich umami flavour profile from the dashi and soy base. Per 100 g, the dish provides 147 kcal, 6.59 g protein, 6.51 g fat, and 15.6 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 358 mg. Dietary fibre is low at 0.89 g per 100 g. The white rice base gives the dish a high glycaemic index (~72) and a high glycaemic load per serving (~39), making it a concern for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal-suitable when prepared with halal-certified chicken and soy sauce. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten (panko breadcrumbs contain wheat).
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken katsu don?
Is chicken katsu don suitable for diabetics?
What is the difference between chicken katsu don and tonkatsu don?
How much sodium is in chicken katsu don?
Can I eat chicken katsu don if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of chicken katsu don?
Is chicken katsu don gluten free?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (F05100019) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogue and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database white rice analogue with full documentation. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a deep-fried breaded chicken rice bowl.
notes: Saturated fat (1.6 g per 100 g) is an adapted estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for a comparable breaded fried chicken and rice composite, as HPB Foodo does not report saturated fat separately for this entry. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these micronutrient and fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish and are not available from a directly equivalent approved source for this composite preparation. The input record listed 'low_calorie' as a dietary tag; this has been removed as 147 kcal per 100 g with a 350 g serving delivering approximately 515 kcal does not meet a standard low-calorie threshold. The input GI value of 0 was unsupported and has been replaced with an analogous estimate of 72 from the University of Sydney GI Database white rice reference. Halal dietary flag is retained from input as plausible for standard restaurant preparation with halal chicken.
